QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_1

question.text: Mr. Arvind transferred a fixed deposit of Rs.10,00,000 to his wife Sunita without any consideration. The FD earns interest of Rs.70,000 during the year. In whose hands will this interest income be taxable?

answer.text: Sunita, as she is the legal owner of the FD after transfer
Split equally between Arvind and Sunita (Rs.35,000 each)
Arvind, as the asset was transferred to spouse without adequate consideration
Neither, as transfers between spouses are exempt under Section 56

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 64(1)(iv), when an asset is transferred to a spouse without adequate consideration, the income from such asset is clubbed with the income of the transferor (Arvind). The clubbing applies to the income, not the asset. **The Trap:** Many candidates confuse legal ownership with tax incidence. Although Sunita legally owns the FD, the income is taxable in Arvind's hands due to clubbing provisions. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A also covers basic clubbing provisions in the context of tax planning for mutual fund investments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When advising married clients on investments in spouse's name, always flag the clubbing provisions — the tax benefit they expect may not materialize.
